title: Plan storage, backup, archive, and retention
purpose: Distinguish active analysis storage, intermediate workflow storage, long-term archive, report storage, backups, disaster recovery, retention, deletion, and cost growth.

whitepaperTarget.title: Practical guidance for building and improving pathogen genomics data and bioinformatics services
whitepaperTarget.primaryAudience: Mixed public-health teams that include laboratory leads, bioinformaticians, data managers, IT or security staff, programme managers, policy staff, funders, and directors.
whitepaperTarget.practicalUse: Help teams move from project-based or retrospective genomics toward a routine, prospective genomic surveillance service with reliable data flow, quality control, workflow provenance, reporting, sharing, storage, support, and continuous improvement.
whitepaperTarget.coreQuestion: Given our public-health use case, organisms, staffing, infrastructure, connectivity, governance constraints, and maturity, what service model and data/infrastructure practices should we put in place next?
successCriteria: Readers can explain why genomics is useful for their public-health decision, and where it cannot substitute for epidemiology, sampling, laboratory quality, metadata, governance, or judgement.
Readers can define the service model they are building: research support, outbreak response, routine surveillance, AMR monitoring, repository submission, clinical support, or a hybrid model.

Readers can map a sample-to-decision data service, including sample receipt, metadata, QC, sequencing, workflow execution, interpretation, reporting, sharing, storage, retention, failures, and feedback loops.
Readers can identify which responsibilities must be owned locally, delegated to partners, or handled through a managed service.
Readers can use the tailored profile to hide irrelevant detail while preserving source-backed recommendations, citations, and visible evidence gaps.
outOfScope: A pathogen-specific QC threshold catalogue unless source-backed conditional material has been extracted.
A universal cloud-first or platform-first recommendation.
A formal accreditation standard or legal compliance manual.
A generic literature review without practical implementation decisions.

synthesisBrief.purpose: Separate storage concepts that are often conflated, and make retention and recovery decisions visible.
synthesisBrief.readerActionAfterReading: Classify each data type as active analysis data, intermediate workflow data, report or record, shared output, archive item, or backup target.
mustCover: Active storage, backup, replication, archive, disaster recovery, retention, and deletion are different decisions.
Raw reads, intermediate files, workflow logs, reports, QC records, reference data, and repository accessions do not need identical rules.
Storage choices affect cost, recovery, auditability, reanalysis, and data sharing.
doNotClaim: Do not define universal retention periods without jurisdictional or institutional source material.
Do not confuse backup with replication or archive.
Do not give storage-size rules without explicitly marking them as context-specific.
knownGaps: Need public-health retention-policy sources before prescribing retention periods.

fragments.title: Storage categories
fragments.text: A genomics service should separate active analysis storage, long-term archive, backup copies, retention policy, access control, and recovery planning. Raw data, filtered data, processed data, reports, workflow logs, QC outputs, repository submissions, and dashboards do not all need the same storage class, access rule, backup schedule, or retention period.
fragments.reviewerNotes: Reviewed against WHO 2023 storage policy, PHE infrastructure, and PHA4GE data-flow text. Detailed retention periods remain a gap.

fragments.title: Storage policy
fragments.text: WHO's national strategy support tool states that storage policy should support short- and long-term analysis and archiving, be defined from available hardware, software, infrastructure and connectivity, and be sized according to the retention strategy for raw, filtered and processed data. It also states that backup locations should be chosen according to data criticality and that data security must apply to local and cloud storage.
fragments.reviewerNotes: Extracted from the public guidance block and aligned to the reviewed storage policy claim.

fragments.title: Service implementation example
fragments.text: The PHE case study shows what this means in a real service: WGS implementation required IT infrastructure to manage and store sequence data, run analysis software, maintain a high-performance computing cluster, provide enough storage and processing speed for service delivery, and use both high-performance and archive storage. Those details are PHE-specific, but the categories are not.
fragments.reviewerNotes: Uses PHE as a source-backed service example without treating its configuration as universal.

fragments.title: Data-flow framing
fragments.text: PHA4GE's data-flow framing links ingestion, storage, processing, delivery, archiving, retention policy, role-based access, provenance, and data-management plans. Treating all data as one storage bucket makes it harder to control cost, recover from failure, govern access, or explain which records must be retained.
fragments.reviewerNotes: Connects storage categories to data flow, access, provenance, and cost control.

fragments.title: Backup as recovery
fragments.text: Backup should be treated as a recovery process. The PHA4GE disaster-recovery note is still rough, but its central question is useful: if the main data store were destroyed, what could the organisation recover, how quickly, and what would be lost? That question should be answered before routine service depends on the system.
fragments.reviewerNotes: Keeps rough disaster-recovery source material as a practical recovery question, not a formal standard.

fragments.title: Local physical resilience
fragments.text: For local servers or local storage, physical resilience is part of the same data service. The PHA4GE source pack's rough power and cooling notes point to secure server-room access, environmental monitoring, dust and temperature control, cooling design, UPS maintenance and generator support as local infrastructure concerns. These should be treated as resilience questions, not as decorative facilities details.
fragments.reviewerNotes: This is only shown where local or hybrid infrastructure is plausible.

fragments.title: Storage, backup and archive serve different jobs
fragments.text: Use this distinction before buying storage or writing retention rules: active storage supports current work, backup supports recovery, archive supports retained records, and repository records support sharing and later linkage.

figure.alt: A service map separating active analysis storage, backup copies, archive storage, repository records, reports, and restore testing.
figure.caption: Use this distinction before buying storage or writing retention rules: active storage supports current work, backup supports recovery, archive supports retained records, and repository records support sharing and later linkage.

fragments.reviewerNotes: Minimal editorial figure already used by the public guidance block.

fragments.title: Storage, backup, archive and recovery map
fragments.text: Use this map to classify genomics data by operational purpose before choosing storage classes, backup schedules or archive rules.

table.title: storage, backup, archive and recovery map
table.summary: Use this map to classify genomics data by operational purpose before choosing storage classes, backup schedules or archive rules.

cells: Active analysis storage
Holds data needed for current workflow execution, QC review, re-runs and short-term troubleshooting.
Owner, access rules, expected working capacity, workflow location, cleanup rule, and what happens when storage fills.
Active storage is not a backup or a long-term archive.
Raw-read archive
Preserves primary sequence data that may support reanalysis, repository submission, correction, validation or future questions.
Retention strategy, archive location, access route, accession linkage, integrity check, and whether data can be regenerated.
The sources support keeping a retention strategy, but not a universal retention period.
Processed outputs and intermediate files
Includes assemblies, consensus genomes, alignments, variant calls, trees, tables and other workflow outputs.
Which outputs are reportable, which are reproducible from raw data, which are shared, and which are discarded after review.
Do not keep every intermediate file by default unless there is a defined audit, validation or reanalysis reason.
Reports, QC records and workflow logs
Supports audit, correction, user communication, quality review, method change review and interpretation of previous results.
Report identifier, workflow and database versions, QC status, correction history, access owner, and retention or archive decision.
Reports and QC records may be more important for audit than large temporary analysis files.
Repository and sharing records
Links local records to public, controlled-access or partner-platform submissions.
Accession or platform ID, upload date, release state, correction or replacement date, withdrawal route, and responsible local owner.
Repository release does not remove the need for local accession tracking and correction history.
Backup copies
Provides recovery after failure, corruption, misconfiguration, accidental deletion or compromise.
What is backed up, where copies are held, version history, schedule, automation, alerts, restore test, and who owns recovery.
Backup is a recovery process, not simply another copy or a substitute for archive policy.
Deletion, expiry or restricted retention
Controls data that should not be kept indefinitely or should be held under special access rules.
Legal or policy basis, data type, owner, deletion approval, deletion log, exception route and effect on reports or accessions.
Deletion rules, retention periods, RTO and RPO need local legal, public-health and institutional review.

fragments.reviewerNotes: This is a category map. Retention periods, RTO, RPO and deletion rules remain jurisdiction-specific gaps.

fragments.title: Setting local retention and recovery targets
fragments.text: Use this worksheet before assigning retention periods, restore-time targets or deletion rules. The sources support the decision structure, not jurisdiction-specific values.

table.title: Setting local retention and recovery targets
table.summary: Use this worksheet before assigning retention periods, restore-time targets or deletion rules. The sources support the decision structure, not jurisdiction-specific values.

cells: Data category and service use
Whether the item is raw data, processed output, report, QC record, workflow log, repository record, dashboard extract, backup copy or archive record; and which public-health product depends on it.
A report used for public-health action, an accessioned raw-read record and a temporary intermediate file do not need the same retention or restore rule.
Do not set one retention rule for the whole genomics folder.
Regeneration and audit need

Whether the item can be regenerated from retained inputs, whether regeneration would reproduce the same result, and whether it is needed for audit, correction, validation, legal review or later reanalysis.
Files that are cheap to regenerate can have different rules from primary data, released reports, QC records or versioned interpretation outputs.
A reproducible workflow still needs the original inputs, versions, parameters and reference data if old results may be reinterpreted.
Criticality and restore priority
Which service stops if the data are unavailable, the maximum tolerable interruption, the maximum tolerable data loss, and who decides priority during recovery.
Backup policy should follow data criticality; a repository-submission log and an active outbreak workspace may need different recovery priority.
Available sources support recording recovery questions, but not universal RTO or RPO values.
Location, access and security
Where the primary copy, archive and backups are held; who can access them; whether data are local, cloud or platform-hosted; and which sensitive fields are restricted.
Storage, backup and archive choices also determine who can access data, who can restore it, and which data-residency or governance rules apply.
Public repository release does not remove local responsibility for sensitive metadata, accession linkage or correction history.
Backup schedule and restore test
Backup frequency, version history, automation, alerting, failure review, restore-test schedule, restore-test result and recovery owner.
A backup that is not monitored or tested may not protect the service when storage fails, files are deleted, or data are corrupted.
This is a recovery checklist, not a formal disaster-recovery or business-continuity runbook.
Deletion, expiry and exception route
Retention period selected locally, legal or policy basis, deletion authority, deletion log, exception or legal-hold route, and effect on reports, accessions and reanalysis.
Some data may need restricted retention or deletion; other records may need to remain linked to released reports and repository accessions.
Numeric retention periods and deletion rules need local legal, public-health, institutional and repository review.

fragments.reviewerNotes: This supports local target-setting but deliberately avoids unsupported retention periods, RTOs or RPOs.

fragments.title: Backup restore-drill record
fragments.text: Use this record to test whether the service can actually recover data needed for reporting, reanalysis, repository correction or service review.

table.title: Backup restore-drill record
table.summary: Use this record to test whether the service can actually recover data needed for reporting, reanalysis, repository correction or service review.

cells: Recovery scenario
What failed or was simulated: accidental deletion, corrupted active storage, unavailable server room, failed cloud or platform access, repository-record loss, or local storage loss.
Whether the drill tests a realistic service failure rather than a generic backup demonstration.
Use the scenario structure, but local business-continuity owners should decide formal drill frequency and severity classes.
Data selected for restore
Data category, sample or run identifiers, report or accession links, QC records, workflow logs, metadata records and whether the item is active, archived, backed up or repository-linked.
Restoring raw reads alone is not enough if reports, QC, workflow versions, metadata or accession links are needed to interpret the result.
Choose examples that represent real service products, not only easy-to-restore test files.
Restore route and owner
Who initiated the restore, who approved it, which backup or archive copy was used, where data were restored, and who verified access after restore.
A backup copy only protects the service if an accountable owner can restore from it and confirm the restored data are usable.
Do not treat replication, object storage versioning or repository submission as proof that local recovery is solved.
Integrity and interpretability check
File integrity, metadata linkage, workflow and database version linkage, report linkage, accession linkage, access permissions, and whether the restored record can support audit or reanalysis.
The restore is only useful if the recovered record can still be interpreted, audited, corrected or reused.
A technically restored file may still be operationally unusable if identifiers, permissions or version records are broken.
Service impact and unresolved risk
What service work stopped during restore, whether reporting, repository submission, reanalysis or user support was affected, unresolved gaps, corrective action owner and review date.
Restore drills should feed service improvement, storage planning, staffing cover, incident routes and recurrent cost decisions.
Do not turn this recovery drill record into a formal RTO or RPO claim without local policy and business-continuity review.

fragments.reviewerNotes: This is a restore-drill record. It does not define restore-time objectives, restore-point objectives, retention periods, formal incident classes, cyber response or business-continuity policy.

fragments.title: Local infrastructure resilience checklist
fragments.text: Use this when storage, compute or backup depend on local servers, local storage rooms or institutional facilities. It is not a formal facilities or business-continuity standard.

table.title: local infrastructure resilience checklist
table.summary: Use this when storage, compute or backup depend on local servers, local storage rooms or institutional facilities. It is not a formal facilities or business-continuity standard.

cells: Server-room or storage-location owner
Physical location, accountable facilities or IT owner, access-control route, visitor or contractor access, and out-of-hours contact.
Data recovery depends on knowing who can physically access and support the local system during an incident.
Do not assume laboratory staff control facilities owned by central IT or estates.
Environmental monitoring
Temperature, humidity or other monitoring available locally, alert recipient, review route, and what service action follows an alert.
Overheating, dust and poor environmental control can turn a storage or compute issue into a service-continuity problem.
Monitoring should be named, but local facilities standards define thresholds.
Power-failure protection
UPS coverage, UPS maintenance owner, generator or longer-outage route, graceful shutdown plan, and systems that remain unavailable during outage.
Local analysis, active storage and backups may fail differently during short and long outages.
Available sources do not support universal UPS sizing, generator capacity or runtime targets.
Backup and restore ownership
Backup job owner, failure alert owner, version history, restore-test date, restore-test result and decision owner during recovery.
A backup copy only protects the service if someone knows it exists, monitors it and can restore from it.
Backup should not be confused with replication or archive; restore testing remains a local responsibility.
Incident escalation
Who is contacted for power, cooling, storage failure, accidental deletion, corruption, cyber incident or unavailable institutional infrastructure.
The reporting service may stop even when sequencing continues if storage, access or recovery routes fail.
This planning checklist does not replace local incident, cyber, disaster-recovery or business-continuity plans.

fragments.reviewerNotes: This is a local-resilience checklist. It does not define formal facilities standards, UPS sizing, generator capacity, RTO/RPO targets, cyber-incident procedures or business-continuity policy.

evidenceChain.claim: A pathogen genomics service should separate active analysis storage, long-term archive, backup, retention policy, access control and recovery planning, because each category serves a different operational purpose.
evidenceChain.limitations: Does not specify retention periods, restore-time objectives, or organism-specific retention rules.

evidence.sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.4 data management
evidence.directQuote: storage requirements should be sized according to the retention strategy chosen
evidence.passageSummary: WHO states that genomic surveillance data-storage policy should support short- and long-term analysis and archiving, be defined from available hardware, software, infrastructure and connectivity, include one or two backup locations depending on criticality, and size storage according to retention strategy for raw, filtered and processed data. The same section says data security must apply to local and cloud storage.
evidence.limitations: Strategy-level guidance; does not define local retention periods or storage sizing formulae.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.2 infrastructure and section 3.3 bioinformatics
evidence.directQuote: A sequencing service requires extensive IT infrastructure
evidence.passageSummary: The PHE case study states that a sequencing service requires IT infrastructure to manage and store sequence data and run analysis software, including expertise for a high-performance computing cluster, maintenance and migration of infrastructure, enough storage and processing speed to avoid service impact, high-performance storage, archive storage, and data-lifecycle management software.
evidence.limitations: Centralised UK service implementation; its specific infrastructure choices should not be generalised as universal requirements.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, sections on data flow, IAM, and scope of responsibility
evidence.directQuote: ingestion, storage processing and delivery
evidence.passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript describes data flow through ingestion, storage, processing and delivery, while satisfying confidentiality, integrity and accessibility. It links data management to storage, access control, versioning, archiving, sharing, provenance, data-management plans, retention policy and role-based access. Its responsibility model separates SaaS, PaaS and IaaS by how much responsibility is handled by users, administrators or external parties.
evidence.limitations: Infrastructure-focused source; not a legal retention policy or detailed disaster-recovery standard.

evidenceChain.claim: Infrastructure planning should assign responsibility for workflows, storage, data movement, access, maintenance, recovery and recurrent costs, rather than treating compute as a one-time hardware purchase.
evidenceChain.limitations: Does not choose a specific compute platform or vendor.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, Five factors for bioinformatics infrastructure
evidence.directQuote: which parts of the pathogen bioinformatics analysis solution do you want to be responsible for?
evidence.passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript introduces five infrastructure questions covering responsibility, workflow management, compute location, data flow, and access.
evidence.limitations: Infrastructure-heavy source; limited coverage of broader governance, repository policy, and economics.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, conclusion
evidence.directQuote: not achieved by CPU cores and terabytes of storage alone
evidence.passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript states that compute infrastructure must include workflow execution engines, data management systems, system configuration, and people to maintain the infrastructure.
evidence.limitations: Supports a general operating-model point, not a complete responsibility matrix.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.2 infrastructure and section 3.3 bioinformatics
evidence.directQuote: A sequencing service requires extensive IT infrastructure
evidence.passageSummary: The PHE case study states that a sequencing service requires IT infrastructure to manage and store sequence data and run analysis software, including expertise for a high-performance computing cluster, maintenance and migration of infrastructure, enough storage and processing speed to avoid service impact, high-performance storage, archive storage, and data-lifecycle management software.
evidence.limitations: Centralised UK service implementation; its specific infrastructure choices should not be generalised as universal requirements.

evidence.sourceLocator: WHO 2024 genomics costing tool user manual, Bioinformatics worksheet section
evidence.directQuote: cloud computing costs, in-house server costs or a hybrid
evidence.passageSummary: WHO's costing manual states that bioinformatics costing can accommodate cloud, in-house server or hybrid models, including software licensing, storage, cloud costs, in-house hardware, maintenance fees, redundancy if selected for storage, and human resources such as system administrators and systems engineers.
evidence.limitations: The manual is SARS-CoV-2 focused in its current version, but the cost categories are useful for recurrent planning.

evidenceChain.claim: A programme using local servers or local storage should include physical infrastructure resilience in its storage and recovery plan, including controlled server-room access, environmental monitoring, cooling, UPS or power-failure planning, backup ownership, restore testing and escalation routes.
evidenceChain.limitations: Does not define formal business-continuity policy, facilities standards, UPS sizing, generator capacity, RTO/RPO targets, retention periods or legal requirements.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHA4GE source pack rough disaster-recovery and power/cooling notes; PHE implementation case study IT infrastructure sections; WHO 2023 data-management and storage sections
evidence.directQuote: secure access-controlled server rooms
evidence.passageSummary: The PHA4GE source pack distinguishes active storage, long-term storage, backup strategy and disaster-recovery planning, and asks whether an organisation could recover if its main data store were destroyed. Its power and cooling notes identify secure access-controlled server rooms, environmental monitoring, dust and temperature control, hot-aisle/cold-aisle layouts, UPS sizing and maintenance, and generator support for longer outages. PHE describes high-performance storage, archive storage, data-lifecycle management software and IT infrastructure as part of operational WGS delivery. WHO's national strategy support tool treats storage, backup, retention, infrastructure, connectivity, local/cloud security and data criticality as part of data-management planning.
evidence.limitations: The PHA4GE disaster-recovery and power/cooling notes are rough source material. This supports checklist questions for local infrastructure resilience, not formal facilities standards, UPS sizing rules, generator specifications, RTO/RPO targets or business-continuity policy.

evidence.sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.4 data management
evidence.directQuote: storage requirements should be sized according to the retention strategy chosen
evidence.passageSummary: WHO states that genomic surveillance data-storage policy should support short- and long-term analysis and archiving, be defined from available hardware, software, infrastructure and connectivity, include one or two backup locations depending on criticality, and size storage according to retention strategy for raw, filtered and processed data. The same section says data security must apply to local and cloud storage.
evidence.limitations: Strategy-level guidance; does not define local retention periods or storage sizing formulae.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.2 infrastructure and section 3.3 bioinformatics
evidence.directQuote: A sequencing service requires extensive IT infrastructure
evidence.passageSummary: The PHE case study states that a sequencing service requires IT infrastructure to manage and store sequence data and run analysis software, including expertise for a high-performance computing cluster, maintenance and migration of infrastructure, enough storage and processing speed to avoid service impact, high-performance storage, archive storage, and data-lifecycle management software.
evidence.limitations: Centralised UK service implementation; its specific infrastructure choices should not be generalised as universal requirements.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, sections on data flow, IAM, and scope of responsibility
evidence.directQuote: ingestion, storage processing and delivery
evidence.passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript describes data flow through ingestion, storage, processing and delivery, while satisfying confidentiality, integrity and accessibility. It links data management to storage, access control, versioning, archiving, sharing, provenance, data-management plans, retention policy and role-based access. Its responsibility model separates SaaS, PaaS and IaaS by how much responsibility is handled by users, administrators or external parties.
evidence.limitations: Infrastructure-focused source; not a legal retention policy or detailed disaster-recovery standard.

evidenceChain.claim: A routine pathogen genomics service should preserve an identifier lineage from sample or isolate through metadata, sequencing, QC, workflow, interpretation, report, repository submission, and later reuse.
evidenceChain.limitations: Supports a general lineage model; field-level organism-specific metadata requirements still need extraction from repository and pathogen-specific sources.

evidence.sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.4 data management
evidence.directQuote: should consider all data relevant to surveillance systems
evidence.passageSummary: WHO states that genomic surveillance data-management policy should not focus only on genetic sequence data, but should consider all data relevant to surveillance systems to support interoperability. The same section links data quality, internal consistency, metadata completeness, metadata quality, storage, privacy/security, and reporting for public-health action.
evidence.limitations: Strategy-level guidance; it does not define a complete local identifier schema.

evidence.sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.4 data scope, 2.7 data provenance, and 2.9 interoperability
evidence.directQuote: auditable trail all along the sequence and metadata generation continuum
evidence.passageSummary: WHO's platform guidance defines minimum metadata as including sample information, high-level sampling strategy, sequencing strategy, bioinformatics methods, and attribution data. It also describes provenance as an auditable trail across sample collection, metadata collection, laboratories, sequencing, bioinformatics, submission, publications, entry route, and identifiers linking records across resources. Its interoperability section supports documented APIs and standards for exchange with other systems.
evidence.limitations: Written for data-sharing platforms; local service databases may need a smaller but compatible internal model.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.3 Workflow development, specimen receipt and management
evidence.directQuote: The interaction between LIMS and the bioinformatics infrastructure
evidence.passageSummary: The PHE case study describes workflow development from sample receipt to reporting and identifies sample tracking, service-user tracking, LIMS interaction with bioinformatics infrastructure, data quality reporting, storage configuration, data access, sample-context integration, and report design as part of the end-to-end service.
evidence.limitations: Centralised UK service example; implementation details may differ in other systems.

evidence.sourceLocator: Armstrong et al. 2019, section on integrating laboratory and epidemiological data
evidence.directQuote: these data need to be integrated
evidence.passageSummary: Armstrong et al. state that laboratory and epidemiological data are often stored and managed separately, and that pathogen genomic data need to be integrated with epidemiological data to realise the value of both.
evidence.limitations: Supports the integration principle; does not prescribe a field-level metadata model.

evidenceChain.claim: Repository and platform choices should be assessed against metadata scope, provenance, identifiers, interoperability, access, curation, and onward use, not only whether sequence files can be uploaded.
evidenceChain.limitations: Platform guidance does not decide which repository is appropriate for every pathogen, jurisdiction, or sensitivity level.

evidence.sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.4 data scope, 2.7 data provenance, and 2.9 interoperability
evidence.directQuote: auditable trail all along the sequence and metadata generation continuum
evidence.passageSummary: WHO's platform guidance defines minimum metadata as including sample information, high-level sampling strategy, sequencing strategy, bioinformatics methods, and attribution data. It also describes provenance as an auditable trail across sample collection, metadata collection, laboratories, sequencing, bioinformatics, submission, publications, entry route, and identifiers linking records across resources. Its interoperability section supports documented APIs and standards for exchange with other systems.
evidence.limitations: Written for data-sharing platforms; local service databases may need a smaller but compatible internal model.

evidenceChain.claim: Costing should include recurrent people, training, quality-management, storage, compute, software, maintenance and support costs, not only sequencers and reagents.
evidenceChain.limitations: Does not support universal cost-per-sample or return-on-investment claims.

evidence.sourceLocator: WHO 2024 genomics costing tool user manual, Bioinformatics worksheet section
evidence.directQuote: cloud computing costs, in-house server costs or a hybrid
evidence.passageSummary: WHO's costing manual states that bioinformatics costing can accommodate cloud, in-house server or hybrid models, including software licensing, storage, cloud costs, in-house hardware, maintenance fees, redundancy if selected for storage, and human resources such as system administrators and systems engineers.
evidence.limitations: The manual is SARS-CoV-2 focused in its current version, but the cost categories are useful for recurrent planning.

evidence.sourceLocator: WHO 2024 genomics costing tool user manual, Personnel and Training and Quality Management worksheet sections
evidence.directQuote: personnel involved in each workflow step
evidence.passageSummary: WHO's costing manual includes personnel and training costs across sequencing workflow steps, administrative and management personnel, quality and biosafety managers, laboratory directors, human resources, and adjustable percentage time. It also includes quality-management costs such as accreditation, proficiency testing, certification and other quality activities.
evidence.limitations: Current manual is SARS-CoV-2 focused and designed for costing, not workforce planning by itself.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.2 infrastructure and section 3.3 bioinformatics
evidence.directQuote: A sequencing service requires extensive IT infrastructure
evidence.passageSummary: The PHE case study states that a sequencing service requires IT infrastructure to manage and store sequence data and run analysis software, including expertise for a high-performance computing cluster, maintenance and migration of infrastructure, enough storage and processing speed to avoid service impact, high-performance storage, archive storage, and data-lifecycle management software.
evidence.limitations: Centralised UK service implementation; its specific infrastructure choices should not be generalised as universal requirements.

evidence.sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, conclusion
evidence.directQuote: not achieved by CPU cores and terabytes of storage alone
evidence.passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript states that compute infrastructure must include workflow execution engines, data management systems, system configuration, and people to maintain the infrastructure.
evidence.limitations: Supports a general operating-model point, not a complete responsibility matrix.

gaps: Need public-health retention-policy sources before prescribing retention periods.
Does not specify retention periods, restore-time objectives, or organism-specific retention rules.
Does not choose a specific compute platform or vendor.
Does not define formal business-continuity policy, facilities standards, UPS sizing, generator capacity, RTO/RPO targets, retention periods or legal requirements.
Supports a general lineage model; field-level organism-specific metadata requirements still need extraction from repository and pathogen-specific sources.
Platform guidance does not decide which repository is appropriate for every pathogen, jurisdiction, or sensitivity level.
Does not support universal cost-per-sample or return-on-investment claims.
Strategy-level guidance; does not define local retention periods or storage sizing formulae.
Centralised UK service implementation; its specific infrastructure choices should not be generalised as universal requirements.
Infrastructure-focused source; not a legal retention policy or detailed disaster-recovery standard.
Infrastructure-heavy source; limited coverage of broader governance, repository policy, and economics.
Supports a general operating-model point, not a complete responsibility matrix.
The manual is SARS-CoV-2 focused in its current version, but the cost categories are useful for recurrent planning.

The PHA4GE disaster-recovery and power/cooling notes are rough source material. This supports checklist questions for local infrastructure resilience, not formal facilities standards, UPS sizing rules, generator specifications, RTO/RPO targets or business-continuity policy.
Strategy-level guidance; it does not define a complete local identifier schema.
Written for data-sharing platforms; local service databases may need a smaller but compatible internal model.
Centralised UK service example; implementation details may differ in other systems.
Supports the integration principle; does not prescribe a field-level metadata model.
Current manual is SARS-CoV-2 focused and designed for costing, not workforce planning by itself.
